coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient and high color rendering index multilayer luminescent solar concentrators based on colloidal carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Jishuai
Wang, Lihua
Jing, Qiang
Zhao, Haiguang
coredata.dc:description: Compared to the silicon-based photovoltaics, large-area luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) can be used as smart PV window for building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPVs). However, because of the low quantum yield of the fluorophores, and reabsorption energy loss, the current obtained large-area LSCs have low optical efficiency. Herein, we demonstrated highly efficient and semitransparent multilayer LSCs based on three types of carbon quantum dots (C-dots). The blue and green C-dots were synthesized using vacuum heating approach and the orange C-dots were synthesized using solvothermal approach. The as-prepared B-, G- and O-C-dots have the QYs of 90 %, 73 % and 72 %, respectively, with large Stokes shift (0.37–0.73 eV). By optimizing the concentration of the C-dots in each layer, the best LSC (100 cm2) with multilayer structure exhibited an external optical efficiency of 9.1 %, and a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 6.0 %, which are higher than that of most of reported LSCs. The simulations indicate that optimized multilayer LSCs could have a theoretical external optical efficiency over 12 % for 1 m2 devices. Besides high optical efficiency, the multilayer LSCs also have high Color Rendering Index (above 80) and high average visible transmission (above 70 %), which make the as-prepared LSCs suitable for potential commercial BIPVs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent solar concentrators
Carbon quantum dots
Multilayer structure
Color rendering index
Optical efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and PL mechanism of C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shandong Natural Science Funds for Distinguished Young Scholar
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: H. Zhao acknowledges the funding support from the Shandong Natural Science Funds for Distinguished Young Scholar (ZR2020JQ20).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)–(c) Typical TEM images and (d)-(f) HRTEM images of the (a), (d) B-C-dots, (b), (e) G-C-dots, and (c), (f) O-C-dots. (g) XPS survey of the C-dots. (h) High resolution XPS C 1 s spectra of the C-dots.

(a) Absorption/emission spectra, (b) QY and (c) PL decay curves of the B-C-dots. The emission spectra were obtained by tuning the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 390 nm. (d) Absorption/emission spectra, (e) QY and (f) PL decay curves of the G-C-dots. The emission spectra were obtained by tuning the excitation wavelengths from 360 to 440 nm. (g) Absorption/emission spectra, (h) QY and (i) PL decay curves of the O-C-dots. The emission spectra were obtained by tuning the excitation wavelengths from 360 to 500 nm. The excitation wavelength is 350, 405 and 450 nm for B-, G- and O-C-dots.
TA measurements of C-dots, for selected time delays; pump @ 365 nm for (a), (c) G-C-dots and 450 nm for (b), (d) O-C-dots. (a)-(b) Map and (c)-(d) TA spectra.

(a) Scheme of two-layer LSC. (b) Scheme of triple-layer LSC. (c) The configuration and working principle of the LSC. (d)-(f) Photographs of the LSCs under room light and (g)-(k) simulated sunlight (AM1.5G, 100 mW/cm2). The LSCs were prepared using (d), (h) B-C-dots, (e), (i) G-C-dots and (f), (j) O-C-dots. Triple-layer LSCs prepared using (g) glass and (k) PMMA upon simulated light. The dimensions of the LSCs are (d)-(j) 10 × 10 × 0.52 cm3 and (k) 20 × 20 × 0.3 cm3.

(a) Spectral irradiance of the triple-layer LSC based on B-/G-/O-C-dots with concentration of 0.32 mg/mL, 1.5 mg/mL and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. (b) AVT of the LSCs based on C-dots with different C-dots concentrations. (c) Calculated ηext of the single-layer or triple-layer LSCs as a function of LSC length or G factor.

(a)-(b) J-V response of silicon PVs attached on the edge of the LSC under the natural sunlight (65 mW/cm2): (a) LSC area of 100 cm2; (b) LSC area of 400 cm2. (c) The chromaticity coordinate of LSCs integrated with B-C-dots, G-C-dots, O-C-dots, B-/G-C-dots, B-/O-C-dots, G-/O-C-dots and B-/G-/O-C-dots. (d)-(e) Transmittance of the LSC using (d) glass and (e) PMMA as waveguide. (f) Integrated PL area of the LSC based on C-dots. Inset of figure f is the photograph of the LSC based on O-C-dots.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient and high color rendering index multilayer luminescent solar concentrators based on colloidal carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics, College of Textiles and Clothes, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physics, College of Textiles and Clothes, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: J.L and L.W contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared to the silicon-based photovoltaics, large-area luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) can be used as smart PV window for building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPVs). However, because of the low quantum yield of the fluorophores, and reabsorption energy loss, the current obtained large-area LSCs have low optical efficiency. Herein, we demonstrated highly efficient and semitransparent multilayer LSCs based on three types of carbon quantum dots (C-dots). The blue and green C-dots were synthesized using vacuum heating approach and the orange C-dots were synthesized using solvothermal approach. The as-prepared B-, G- and O-C-dots have the QYs of 90 %, 73 % and 72 %, respectively, with large Stokes shift (0.37–0.73 eV). By optimizing the concentration of the C-dots in each layer, the best LSC (100 cm2) with multilayer structure exhibited an external optical efficiency of 9.1 %, and a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 6.0 %, which are higher than that of most of reported LSCs. The simulations indicate that optimized multilayer LSCs could have a theoretical external optical efficiency over 12 % for 1 m2 devices. Besides high optical efficiency, the multilayer LSCs also have high Color Rendering Index (above 80) and high average visible transmission (above 70 %), which make the as-prepared LSCs suitable for potential commercial BIPVs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPVs) have attracted a lot of attentions for harvesting the renewable solar energy. Compared to silicon-based photovoltaics (PVs), luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have been used for large-area semi-transparent solar-to-electricity conversion as “smart” windows [1–4]. Typically, an LSC consists of an optical waveguide embedded with fluorophores, such as dyes/polymers, quantum dots, perovskites and carbon quantum dots (C-dots) [5–13]. By absorbing part of the sunlight, the fluorophores can re-emit light through fluorescence, which was guided to the edges of the waveguide because of the total internal reflection (TIR). The PV coupled on the edges of an LSC absorb the concentrated fluorescence and convert them to electricity. LSC technology could be potential use for the BIPVs, especially for the semitransparent glass windows or roofs. However, there are still several obstacles before the real commercial application, including (i) the low external optical efficiency of the LSCs; (ii) the transparency of the LSC after integration with windows or roofs; (iii) the cost of the LSCs and (iv) the long-term operation stability of the LSCs.

The theoretical external optical efficiency (η ext) of an LSC is defined as the number of photons emitted from the four edges of an LSC over the total number of photons impinging on the top surface of an LSC, which can be calculated as: [2]
(1)
η
e x t
=
( 1 - R )
η
T I R
∙
η
a b s
∙
η
Q Y
∙
η
S t o k e s
∙
η
h o s t
∙ η
s e l f
where R is the reflection of light from the waveguide surface, η TIR is the TIR efficiency, η abs is the absorbed fraction of solar light, η QY is the quantum yield (QY) of fluorophores, η Stokes is the fraction of energy remaining after the fluorescence process, η host is the transport efficiency of the wave-guided photons through the waveguide, and η self is the re-absorption of the emitted photons by another fluorophore in the waveguide. Typically, the η ext is strongly dependent on the absorption portion and QY of the fluorophores [2,3]. While, because the typical size of the LSC for the real commercialization reaches over one square meter, the η ext of the LSC is also strongly dependent on the spectral overlap between the absorption and emission of the fluorophores [1]. Large spectral overlap leads to the strong reabsorption energy losses, resulted in low η ext of the large-area LSCs. Researchers have predicted that the LSC technology can have similar price of the electricity compared to PVs if the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the LSC-PV system is over 6 % [14]. However, the current obtained η ext of the large-area LSCs (>100 cm2) is still low [less than 6.8 % as shown in Table S1, Supporting Information] [15–19].

The current research efforts to achieve high optical efficiency LSCs are still focused on the development of efficient fluorophores, which need to have broad absorption, high QY and large Stokes shift. Compared to widely reported dyes/polymers and quantum dots [1], very recently, C-dots have been used for LSC fabrication [16–19]. Unlike quantum dots, the C-dots do not contain metal elements and are composed of a small crystalline graphitized core and surface functional groups with main composition of C, N and O. They have attracted much attention for optical and electrical applications, because of their high QY, tunable absorption/emission, low cost and relatively simple synthetic methods [20,21]. C-dots have been used as high-quality light converters for the LSCs [15–19]. For example, Zhou et al reported the tandem LSCs (100 cm2) based on C-dots exhibit a η ext of 1.1 % under natural sun irradiation (70 mW/cm2) [22]. Liu et al. reported the tandem LSCs (100 cm2) based on red C-dots and green C-dots exhibited a η ext of 6.8 % under natural sun irradiation (70 mW/cm2) [16]. Li et al. reported the laminated LSC (100 cm2) using the yellow emitting C-dots achieves a η ext of 4.56 % and PCE of 4.1 % under natural sunlight (45 mW/cm2) [19]. We summarized the recent results of the LSCs based on C-dots and other fluorophores in Table S1 (Supporting Information). The current obtained ηext of the solid-state C-dots based LSCs is still lower than 7 % for large-area LSCs (over 100 cm2) with single layer, laminated or tandem structure.

Besides the demand of high optical efficiency, an LSC needs to transmit high-quality light to create a pleasant indoor environment when used in a transparent or semi-transparent window. To assess the quality of light, the Colour Rendering Index (CRI) and average visible light transmissivity (AVT) are generally considered to be the leading metric for assessing window applications [23]. Therefore, balancing the optical efficiency, CRI and transparency is important for the potential BIPV application of the LSC, which is still lack of investigation.

In this work, we demonstrated highly efficient and high CRI transparent multilayer LSCs based on three types of C-dots. The blue and green C-dots (B-C-dots and G-C-dots) were synthesized using vacuum heating approach and the orange C-dots (O-C-dots) were produced via solvothermal approach. The as-prepared B-, G- and O-C-dots have the QYs of 90 %, 73 % and 72 %, respectively, with large Stokes shifts (0.37 – 0.73 eV) to minimize the reabsorption energy loss. By optimizing the concentration of the C-dots in each layer, the best LSC (100 cm2) exhibited an η ext of 9.1 %, and a PCE of 6.0 % under natural sunlight illumination (65 mW/cm2). Besides, the multilayer LSCs based on the B-/G-/O-C-dots have high CRI (above 80) and high AVT (above 70 %), which make the as-prepared LSCs suitable for potential commercial BIPVs.

The B-C-dots were prepared via a vacuum heating approach, which has been used to prepare green emitting C-dots [17,24]. Typically, 3 g citric acid (CA) and 1.5 g L-cysteamine hydrochloride were mixed together and dispersed in 5 mL water. The clear solution was transferred to the 100 mL flask and heated to 120 °C under vacuum. After totally pumped out the water, the slurry was further heated to 180 °C under vacuum. After 2 h heating at 180 °C, the solid powder was collected by adding 100 mL of ethanol by sonication. Without using the vacuum, with the identical reaction parameters, the as-prepared product is highly carbonization with black color, without showing any of the fluorescence.

The G-C-dots were also prepared by a modified vacuum heating method, without using the CaCl2 [17,24]. For a typical reaction, 3 g CA and 9 g urea were mixed together and dispersed in 5 mL water. After sonication, the clear solution was transferred to the 100 mL flask and heated to 80 °C under vacuum. After 30 min, the reaction temperature was increased to 120 °C, until all water was pumped away. The temperature was further increased to 180 °C and maintained at this temperature for 3 h. After cooling the temperature to 25 °C naturally, the solid powder was dispersed to 100 mL ethanol by sonication. We found that without adding CaCl2, we can also produce the high quality green emitting C-dots. While the as-prepared C-dots do not contain Ca, enables the simple purification procedure.

For the synthesis of the O-C-dots and red colored C-dots, we used a solvothermal approach using 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid, and 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride as precursors. In details, 0.45 g 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid and 0.02 g 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride were mixed together and further dispersed in 50 mL of N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF). The mixture was transferred into 100 mL autoclave and put into the oven with temperature of 200 °C for 4–12 h. The autoclave was naturally cooled to 25 °C and opened to take the mixture.

For the purification, all types of C-dots mixture were purified using chromatographic silica columns [25]. A mixture of methanol and CH2Cl2 (methanol: CH2Cl2 = 1:10) was used to form silica gel. The obtained mixture was centrifuged at 9000 r.p.m. for 10 min to obtain the clear supernatant. The as-obtained supernatant was transferred into dialysis bags with a molecular weight of 500 Da for 12 h. For the B-C-dots mixture, the mixture outside the dialysis bag was picked up and for the G-/O- C-dots, the mixture inside the dialysis bag was kept. The obtained supernatant was further freeze-dried to obtain C-dots powder. The obtained C-dots powder was redispersed in ethanol with different concentrations for further characterizations or device fabrication.

The laminated structure LSCs were prepared via a drop-casting approach. In details, 15 mg C-dots, 2 g PVP K30 and 10 mL ethanol were mixed together. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min to obtain a clear solution without bubbles. The mixture was further centrifuged at 12,000 r.p.m. for 10 min to remove any of the aggregations or bubbles. Finally, the obtained supernatant was drop-casted on the surface of the glass or PMMA plate with different dimensions (10 × 10 × 0.5 cm3, 20 × 20 × 0.28 cm3) and dried at room condition for 1 h until all the solvent was evaporated. The thickness of the C-dots/PVP film was measured to be ∼ 100 um. Subsequently, the commercial epoxy glue (double component) was prepared by mixing the component A and component B with weight ratio of 1:2. After fully mixing the component A and B, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 r.p.m. for 10 min. The obtained mixture without bubbles was drop-casting on the dried C-dots/PVP layer by glass rod. The as-measured layer of the epoxy has the average thickness of ∼ 100 um. Thicker epoxy layer will largely reduce the transparency of the LSC. After 24 h dying at ambient condition, the laminated LSC was obtained and used for further characterizations.
For the preparation of the multilayer LSC, the first, second and third layer is the LSC based on B-C-dots, G-C-dots and O-C-dots, respectively. The distance between each layer was set as ∼ 2 cm.

The morphology and structure of the three types of C-dots were carried out using a JEOL 2100F TEM. FTIR (Nicolet 6700) and XPS (ESCALAB 250Xi) were used to characterize the surface functional groups of the C-dots. Absorption spectra were acquired with a UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Static and transient PL measurements and QYs were performed on an Edinburgh FLS980 instrument equipped with pulsed LEDs and integrating sphere. The refractive index of glass, PMMA, PVP/C-dots film, epoxy film was measured using a WAY-2S Albert refractometer (INEA). The femtosecond transient absorption (fs-TA) measurements were carried out by ultrafast systems (helios) with time resolution of 100 fs. The measurements were performed in a 1 mm pathlength cuvette containing the C-dots dispersed in ethanol at ambient condition.

The ηext of the LSCs was measured under natural light (uncharacterized spectrum, 65 mW/cm2, whose intensity was directly measured by using a commercial calibrated Zolix QE-B1 solar cell). The power meter (Newport Model 843-R) was directly coupled on the one edge of the LSC for ηext measurement. The calibrated commercial PV cells was used to measure the PCE of the LSCs. One edge of the LSC was fully covered by the PV cells. Unattached area of the PV was covered by the black tape. We cannot measure the optical efficiency of the LSCs by using commercial sunlight simulator as the LSC is very large (100–400 cm2). Instead, we directly use the natural sunlight for the measurement. To avoid the large difference in the solar spectrum by the weather, we measured all the samples in one day at noon (May, Qingdao China) with a light intensity of 65 mW/cm2. During the measurement, we found the optical efficiency of the LSC is dependent on the configuration of the LSC. The light directly illuminating on the C-dots/polymer gives the highest efficiency. In this work, for all the measurement, the light was shined on the epoxy/C-dots/polymer layer, other than the glass or PMMA side.

The stability of LSCs was measured by home-made PL spectrometer upon illumination (365 nm light emitting device, 500 mW/cm2) in ambient conditions at 25 °C with humidity of 50 %. The chromaticity coordinate and CRI of LSCs were measured by HPCS-320 Spectral Illuminance Metea upon natural sunlight (65 mW/cm2).

The B-/G-C-dots were prepared using vacuum heating approach using citric acid/L-cysteamine hydrochloride or citric acid/urea as precursors, respectively (see the Experimental Section).Benefiting from the vacuum conditions, we can produce very bright blue and green emitting C-dots. The O-C-dots were synthesized via a solvothermal approach using 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid, and 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride as precursors. By varying the reaction time, the as-prepared C-dots exhibited bright orange and red colors. The as-prepared C-dots were further purified by silica gel column chromatography and dialysis.

The as-produced C-dots have a uniform size distribution of 4.0 ± 1.8 nm (B-C-dots), 4.3 ± 1.6 nm (G-C-dots) and 5.0 ± 1.6 nm (O-C-dots), as shown in Fig. 1 a–c and Fig. S1. As shown in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), each of the individual C-dot exhibited a highly crystalline structure with the measured d spacing of 2.1–2.4 Å (Fig. 1d–f). d spacing of 2.4 and 2.1 Å, corresponds to the (112) and (100) plane of graphite carbon, respectively [17]. The TEM result indicates that the as-synthesized C-dots have a highly crystalline core. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicates the presence of C, N and O in the B-/G-C-dots (Fig. 1g). The O-C-dots contain C and O elements and the presence of tiny N might be due to the contamination as the precursors used to produce O-C-dots do not contain N element. The high resolution C1s and N1s spectra indicated the formation of the C = O, C-OH and N-H group in the B-C-dots (Fig. 1h and Fig. S2) [21]. The G-C-dots have COOH, C-OH and N-H and the O-C-dots have the C = O and C-OH bonds on the surface (Fig. 1h and Fig. S2). The XPS results are consistent with the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) data (Fig. S3). Based on the TEM, XPS and FTIR results, we conclude that all C-dots have a crystalline core and functionalized with surface groups which provide the good water solubility [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and PL mechanism of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared B-C-dots have the absorption ranging from 300 to 400 nm with the first excitonic absorption peak centered at 343 nm (Fig. 2 a). The emission peak was centered at 495 nm with a Stokes shift of 0.73 eV (87 nm) (Fig. 2a). The as-synthesized G-C-dots have the absorption ranging from 300 to 500 nm with the first excitonic absorption peak centered at 413 nm and emission peak of 516 nm with a Stokes shift of 0.59 eV (103 nm) (Fig. 2d). The O-C-dots can absorb the sun spectrum from 300 to 600 nm with the first excitonic absorption peak of 500 nm (Fig. 2g). The emission peak was located at 584 nm with a Stokes shift of 0.37 eV (84 nm) (Fig. 2g). For the three types of C-dots, they show an excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2a, d, g. There is no significant variation for the emission peak by tuning the wavelengths of the excitation. Together with their large Stokes shift, we deduced that the PL behavior was dominated by the inter-band energy states. The B-/G-/O-C-dots have a strong blue, green and orange emission upon UV illumination (Fig. S4). Consistently, the as-obtained B-/G-/O-C-dots have the QYs of 90 %, 73 % and 72 % (Fig. 2b, e, h), respectively. The PL shoulder located at 470 nm in Fig. 2h might be due to the magnification of the background signal in the log scale, without any sign of PL in the PL spectra (Fig. 2g). We further measured the lifetimes of the C-dots in ethanol. The typical lifetime is 10.2 ns (B-C-dots), 14.0 ns (G-C-dots) and 5.5 ns (O-C-dots) (Fig. 2c, f, i). The lifetime of the C-dots is usually determined by their energy structure and surface defects/traps states, which govern their radiative decay rate and non-radiative rate [26]. The obtained C-dots have the lifetime values falling within the typical values ranging from several ns to several tens ns, which are in accordance with the lifetimes in most reports about carbon dots [17,27]. Besides the O-C-dots during the solvothermal reaction, we can produce the red-emitting C-dots with emission peak located at 650 nm (Fig. S5). The red C-dots (3 mg/mL) have the QY of only 8.4 % in ethanol because of high-concentration induced quenching (Fig. S5). By diluting the original concentration of C-dots to 0.15 mg/mL, we obtained the red-emitting C-dots with QY of 30.1 % in ethanol (Fig. S5). While this QY is still low for fabricating high-efficiency LSCs. Only the blue, green and orange C-dots are selected for LSC fabrication.

To reveal the exciton dynamic of the C-dots, we performed time resolved ultra-fast transient absorption spectroscopy (fs-TA) measurements with a time resolution of 100-fs and excitation wavelength of 300 nm (B-C-dots), 365 nm (G-C-dots) and 450 nm (O-C-dots) with broad visible probe. Fig. 3 a, b and Fig. S6a display the contour plots. We can clearly see the well separation between the photoinduced absorption (PIA) to higher excited states and the simulated emission (SE) or excited state absorption (ESA), indicating the interband-dominated band structure (Fig. S7a–c). As shown in Fig. S6, the negative band (320–380 nm) is due to the PIA, consistently with the absorption of the B-C-dots. The band between 400 and 550 nm is due to the SE with the peak centered at 430 nm, similar with the PL peak of the B-C-dots. The well separation of the PIA and SE peak, further confirming a large Stokes shift. We found even at 0.2 ps, we can observe the SE signal, which does not vary as a function of decay time, indicating that the Stokes shift in B-C-dots is very stable and takes place on a timescale much shorter than 0.2 ps. For the G-C-dots, as shown in Fig. 3c, three peaks were found after excitation at 365 nm. The negative at 400 nm is consistent with their absorption peak (405 nm); the positive peak cantered at 450 nm because of the ESA; In the wavelength ranging from 470 to 600 nm, the peak located at 520 nm is due to the SE, indicating a very large Stokes shift of 0.71 eV, similar as the value calculated from their static-state absorption and PL spectrum (0.73 eV). The large Stokes shift of the G-C-dots is similar as the value reported previously using the citric acid/urea and CaCl2, indicating that without using the CaCl2, by using the vacuum heating approach and carefully optimizing the reaction parameters, we still can produce the high-quality green C-dots [17]. Throughout our 5 ns temporal window we observe that the SE band does not lose significant intensity, consistent with the high QY and long lifetime 14.3 ns. The PIA and ESA signals show a fast decay (0.2 and 0.64 ps) compared to a long decay (0.5 ns) of the SE (Fig. S8a). Similarly, we also observed the negative peak (500 nm) of the PIA signal in O-C-dots and the ESA peak position is around 620 nm. The peak position of the PIA and ESA does not show a significant varication at different decay times, indicating the stable band-structure of the O-C-dots. In contrast to the fast decay (70 ps) for the PIA signal, the ESA signal in O-C-dots show the decay time up to 2.5 ns (Fig. S8b), with similar range as their PL lifetime (5.8 ns), explaining that why we did not observe the SE signal in the wavelength range of 500–700 nm. The strong competition between the ESA and SE process leads to the undetected SE signal. It is worth to highlight that in the TA measurement, we used a high concentration (3 mg/mL), leading to the aggregation-induced PL quenching. Based on the static and transient PL and TA measurements, we proposed the band structure and exciton dynamic in three types of C-dots as shown in Fig. S7a–c. Upon excitation, the electrons jumped to the excited energy states and further decayed to the lowest occupied molecular orbital (LOMO). The electrons further decayed to the interband energy state and recombined with the holes by releasing the photons. The stable interband energy state explains not only the high QY and excitation-independent behavior, but also the large Stokes shift. We believe the interband energy state is due to the highly carbonization core and well-packing surface functional groups.

Aiming to achieve high efficiency LSC, in this work, a multilayer structure was proposed as shown in Fig. 4 a, b. The multilayer configuration enables to improve the absorption efficiency and also enables to design a multiple-layer window, which can switch the layer for the light absorption or transmission [28,29]. In this “smart” window, we can choose all layers for light absorption to obtain the maximum optical efficiency; Or we can choose two of three or one of three layers to modulate the CRI and maintain the transmittance of the window. The single layer LSC was prepared by using drop-casting approach [30]. Typically, the C-dots solution was mixed with polymer and further drop-casted on the glass or PMMA. Due to the C-dots/polymer layer could be detached from the waveguide, a laminated structure was used for the preparation of the single LSC as shown in Fig. 4c. The C-dots ethanol solution (10 mL, 15 mg/mL) was mixed with PVP polymer (K30, 200 mg/mL) and drop-casted on the glass or PMMA. After drying at ambient conditions, the double-component epoxy glue was drop-casted on the C-dots/polymer layer with a thickness of ∼ 100 um. The typical dimension of the glass for the LSC is 10 × 10 × 0.5 cm3 and the dimension of the PMMA plate is 20 × 20 × 0.28 cm3. The calculated geometric factor (G) is equal to L/4d, where L is the length of the LSC and d is the overall thickness of the LSC. For the glass based LSC, the G factor is 4.8 and that of the LSC based on PMMA is 16.6. The as-prepared LSCs based on B-/G-/O-C-dots are very transparent, and we can clearly see the words behind the LSCs (Fig. 4d, e, f). Upon the simulated sunlight illumination, we can see the concentrated blue, green or orange light on the edges of the LSCs (Fig. 4h, i, j). Even putting three LSCs together, the triple-layer LSC is still transparent (Fig. 4g). Similar phenomenon was also found for the large area triple-layer LSC based on PMMA (Fig. 4k). We did not find any significant variation after the C-dots were embedded in the polymer, evidenced by the unchanged lifetimes (Fig. 2c, f, i). Distance-dependent PL measurement is an efficient approach to evaluate the reabsorption energy loss [16]. The home-made set-up was shown in Fig. S9. By fixing the position of the detector and the LSC, we tune the position of the excitation beam to tune the light path (distance between the excitation and detector). As shown in Fig. S9b, very slight PL spectral shift was observed for three types of C-dots based LSCs, indicating the reabsorption energy loss does not play a significant role, benefiting from (i) large Stokes shift (small spectral overlap between the absorption and emission) and (ii) optimal C-dots concentration (0.25 – 1 wt%), which prohibits the energy transfer [28,29].

Based on the measured absorption/PL spectra and QYs of the as-prepared three types C-dots, we simulated the absorption, AVT and ηext of the LSCs (details included in the Supporting Information) [21,31]. The B-C-dots can absorb 2.9 % of the solar energy; the G-C-dots can absorb 14.3 % of the solar energy and O-C-dots can absorb 21 % of the solar energy (Fig. 5 a). These values are consistent with their absorption spectrum. As clearly indicated in Fig. 5a, the G-C-dots can absorb the portion in the range of 340–450 nm, which the O-C-dots cannot fully absorb, indicating that the multilayer configuration could enhance the absorption efficiency of the LSCs. As three types of C-dots can all absorb the solar energy ranging from 300 − 400 nm, the QY and Stokes shift of the emitter determine the optical efficiency of the LSCs. In our case, the QY of the B-C-dots (90 %) is higher than G-/O-C-dots (73 % and 72 %), thus the use of B-C-dots gives the highest optical efficiency in the UV range. Consistently, as shown in Fig. 5c, the η ext of the LSC using B-C-dots slightly drops with the increase of the length of the LSC, from 1.95 % (1 cm) to 1.91 % (100 cm), benefiting from its large Stokes shift and high QY. For the G-C-dots, when L is 100 cm,
η
e x t
keeps 86 % of its initial value (7.75 %). The LSC based O-C-dots maintains 89 % of its initial value (10.74 %). By optimizing the absorption of the three types of C-dots based on their optical properties, we calculated the ηext of the triple-layer LSC. From the top to the bottom, the B-C-dots, G-C-dots and O-C-dots were used to fabricate the triple-layer LSC. Considering the solar energy loss due to the surface reflection (4 %), the first layer can absorb 2.8 %, the second layer can absorb 11 % and the third layer can absorb 12.6 % of solar energy. Overall, in the triple-layer LSC, the ηext can reach 11.9 %, when the L is 100 cm (G of 50). This value indicates that the triple-layer LSC can be great candidate for large-area window used in BIPVs [1].

We further simulated the AVT of the single layer LSC based on C-dots [17]. The absorption range was calculated from 430 nm to 670 nm. The method for AVT calculation was included in Equation S5 (Supporting Information). Typically, with the increase of concentration, the LSC based on the C-dots can absorb more solar energy as shown in Fig. S10a–c. As shown in Fig. 5b, the LSC based on B-C-dots shows the AVT of 96 %, the LSC based on G-C-dots have the AVT of 71 % with the C-dots concentration as high as 5 mg/mL. However, because of the strong visible range absorption, the AVT of the LSC based on the O-C-dots decreases strongly with the increase of the C-dots concentration until 1.6 mg/mL. Further increase of the C-dots concentration does not contribute to the decrease of the AVT, as in the simulation, we only consider the effect of the absorption of the C-dots, without considering the scattering effect of the C-dots/polymer layer. For single-layer LSC based on O-C-dots, the highest ηext can reach 9.2 % (1 m2), with the AVT is only 51 %. In the triple-layer configuration, the LSC based on the O-C-dots can have the AVT over 75 % with C-dots concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, while keeping the high overall ηext (triple-layer LSC: 12 %, 1 m2). In this way, we could balance the optical efficiency and the AVT of the LSC. Adjusting the concentration in each layer offers us a great opportunity to achieve high optical efficiency, meanwhile keeping the suitable AVT.

We further measured the η ext of the LSC by coupling a power meter directly at the edge of the LSC. η ext was calculated as: [2]
(2)
η
e x t
=
P
o u t
∙
A
e d g e
P
i n
∙
A
L S C
=
P
o u t
P
i n
∙ G
Where P out is the power intensity of the LSC on the edge, P in is the power intensity of the LSC on the surface under direct illumination, A edge is the area of the edges of the LSC, A LSC is the area of the top of the LSC and G is calculated to be 4.8 for 10 × 10 × 0.52 cm3, and 16.6 for 20 × 20 × 0.3 cm3 LSC. The LSCs were measured using the natural sunlight (noon, May, Qingdao, with power intensity of 65 mW/cm2). The sunlight intensity was measured using a calibrated solar cell. The measured ηext are 9.1 % (G = 4.8, 100 cm2) and 3.5 % (G = 16.6, 400 cm2) for large-area triple-layer LSCs, which is consistent with the trend of the calculated ηext of the LSCs with different G or dimensions. The lower absolute values of the ηext may be due to the scattering effect, as we cannot make the uniformity of the C-dots/polymer layer and the epoxy cover layer. The obtained ηext of the triple-layer LSC is obviously higher than each of the ηext of the single layer LSC (1.85 % of B-dots; 6.5 % of G-dots; 6.4 % of O-dots). The obtained ηext of the large-area solid-state triple-layer LSC is the highest ever reported for large area LSCs (dimensions > 100 cm2) based on C-dots (Table S1). Considering the absorption of ∼ 20 % of the solar energy by the triple-layer LSC, the concentration factor is calculated to be 2.6 based on the equation of Pout/(Pin × 20 %), indicating the concentration effect in the LSC. Where P out is the generated power intensity on the edge, P in is the absorbed power intensity.

We also measured the PCE of the LSCs by using a calibrated silicon PV cell. The natural sunlight intensity was measured by using a commercial calibrated Zolix QE-B1 solar cell. Under natural sunlight (May, Qingdao, China), the glass plate coupled with PV exhibited a current density of 0.1 mA/cm2 upon light and 0.05 mA/cm2 in the dark. For the triple-layer LSC-PV, the current density is 0.22 mA/cm2 for the light measurement and 0.12 mA/cm2 for dark measurement. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 6 a, the LSC based on O-C-dots with the dimension of 100 cm2 exhibits J sc as high as 26.5 mA/cm2 (65 mW/cm2), which is significantly higher than the control glass-PV. The LSC (100 cm2) exhibits a PCE as high as 1.9 %, 3.6 % and 3.6 % for B-, G- and O-C-dots based LSC, respectively (Fig. 6a). The large area LSC using PMMA (400 cm2) has a PCE of 0.8 %, 1.2 % and 1.1 % for B-, G- and O-C-dots based LSC, respectively (Fig. 6b). With the triple-layer configuration, the LSC exhibited a PCE of 6.0 % (100 cm2) and 2.1 % (400 cm2) (Fig. 6a, b). This is the highest PCE for the solid-state LSC based on the colloidal C-dots. Similar as reported in the literature [14,27,28], the triple-layer structure strongly improves the PCE of the LSC. As shown in the Fig. 6f (inset), the single layer LSC based on the O-C-dots used for the bottom layer of triple-layer LSC show a very well transmittance as we can clearly see the landscape through the LSC. We further quantitatively measure the transmittance of each layer of the triple-layer LSC. As shown in Fig. 6d, the pure glass has the transmittance of 90 %. Due to the absorption of the LSC, the layer based on B-, G- and O-C-dots show the transmittance of 84 %, 74 % and 70 %, respectively (Fig. 6d). Similarly transparent trend was observed for the LSC using the PMMA. The PMMA plate (0.28 cm) exhibited a transmittance of 92.4 %, and the LSC based on the B-, G- and O-C-dots shows the transmittance of 78 %, 72 % and 73 %, respectively (Fig. 6e). Although the LSC-based on PMMA has a lighter weight compared to glass, the PMMA strongly absorb the sunlight in the range of 300–400 nm (∼80 %). Thus, for obtaining higher efficiency, glass is better than the PMMA matrix if the light is shining on the PMMA side, other than the C-dots/polymer side. Together with the high PCE, the above data indicate that the LSCs based on C-dots are very suitable for used in “smart” window with high optical efficiency, meanwhile keeping suitable AVT [31,32]. Besides, we measured the chromaticity coordinate and CRI of the LSCs with single, tandem or triple-layer. As shown in Fig. 6c, under natural sunlight (65 mW/cm2), the chromaticity coordinate of LSCs integrated with a) B-C-dots, b) G-C-dots, c) O-C-dots, d) B-/G-C-dots, e) B-/O-C-dots, f) G-/O-C-dots and g) B-/G-/O-C-dots is (0.345, 0.355), (0.394, 0.452), (0.361, 0.360), (0.388, 0.448), (0.360, 0.364), (0.409, 0.458), (0.407, 0.459), respectively, corresponding to the CRI of 99, 82, 87, 83, 97, 81, 81, respectively. The high CRI can fulfill the requirement of window.

The as-prepared LSCs are very stable benefiting from the sealing of the epoxy glue. After storing the LSC at ambient condition (25 °C, humidity, 60 %), we did not find any loss of the optical efficiency after 3-month storage. To verify the long-term operation stability, we directly measured the integrated PL area change by using a PL spectrometer. A high power density of the light emitting devices (395 nm, 20 W/cm2) was used as light resource. After 15 h illumination, the LSC based on B-/G-/O-C-dots maintained 90 %, 91 % and 96 % of its initial value, respectively (Fig. 6f), indicating that the as-prepared LSC has a very well photostability, suitable for long-term operation.

In summary, we demonstrated an efficient and transparent multilayer LSC based on three types of C-dots. The as-prepared B-, G- and O-C-dots have a highly crystalline core with functional surface groups, showing the QYs of 90 %, 73 % and 72 %, respectively, with large Stokes shift (0.37 – 0.73 eV). The transient PL and TA spectroscopy have revealed the exciton dynamic of the C-dots. The stable surface-state dominant energy state determined the inter-band emission, explaining the high QYs and large Stokes shift of the C-dots. The simulations indicate that optimized triple-layer LSCs have a ηext over 12 % for 1 m2 devices. The as-prepared large-area LSCs (100 cm2) exhibited a ηext as high as 8 % with controllable AVT by optimizing the concentration of the C-dots. The individual layer of the LSC based on the B-/G-/O-C-dots has the typical AVT of over 70 % and high CRI (above 80), which makes the as-prepared LSCs suitable as “smart” window or roofs for BIPVs. The as-prepared C-dots can also be a promising candidate as building blocks for the optoelectronic devices, such as light emitting devices, display and excitonic solar cells.
Jishuai Lin: Investigation. Lihua Wang: Investigation. Qiang Jing: Writing – review & editing. Haiguang Zhao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. Zhao acknowledges the funding support from the Shandong Natural Science Funds for Distinguished Young Scholar (ZR2020JQ20).
